summary: Stancil argues that the business world has overcorrected against intuition by treating emotions as inadmissible evidence, even though feelings often act as leading indicators of what data will later confirm. He contends that companies should surface emotions alongside analytics to make more honest decisions, avoid self-deception, and unlock bolder moves. Emotions shouldn’t drive choices blindly, but they should inform them as real signals.
key_points: Correlation and emotion are imperfect but useful leading indicators that should not be dismissed as mere noise.
Analytical culture often masks emotion with quantitative jargon, which distorts decision-making rather than improving it.
Teams ignore gut signals at their peril; many failures stem from trusting spreadsheets over instincts.
Openly acknowledging emotions can make discussions more honest and reduce hidden bias in decision-making.
Emotions provide conviction and courage that data alone cannot supply.
money_quotes: Correlation may not imply causation, but it’s almost always its leading indicator.
There’s a lot of signal in our emotions, even if it’s not scientifically robust or “statistically significant.”
They die because they assume their numbers are right and their emotions are wrong.
The best companies and leaders aren’t driven by emotions, but they don’t ignore them either.
But the next great idea is in these feelings—and data alone will never give us the courage to move on them.
key_insight: Treat emotions as informative signals alongside data, because honest, courageous decisions require both analysis and feeling.
